summary: Jeff Atwood announces Stack Overflow Careers, a CV hosting and job matching service built on top of the Stack Overflow community. He reflects on Stack Overflow's first year, emphasizing that the platform's success comes entirely from the programmers who participate, not from the team behind it. The new careers product aims to fix what Atwood and Joel Spolsky see as broken hiring practices by connecting engaged programmers with companies that truly value engineering talent. The service offers free public CV hosting with optional paid features for job seekers and substantial fees for employers, inverting the typical job-seeking dynamic so companies pursue programmers rather than the reverse.
key_points: Stack Overflow's value comes from its community of participating programmers, not from its creators or code
The 'pay it forward' model of programmers helping peers creates a permanently archived knowledge base for future developers
Current programmer hiring practices (Monster, Dice, etc.) are broken and reduce candidates to acronym-matching
Stack Overflow Careers inverts the job-seeking dynamic: companies pursue programmers, not the other way around
Teaching new programmers is essential—'the future of our entire profession depends on it'
money_quotes: The community is far, far smarter than I will ever be.
Bad code that isn't written, is bad code that another poor programmer won't have to debug.
The future of our entire profession depends on it.
Joel and I believe current hiring practices for programmers are incredibly broken.
Our goal isn't to put warm bodies in front of interviewers. Our goal is to create love connections.
In short, Stack Overflow Careers amplifies your awesome.
key_insight: A thriving developer community creates value that can be channeled into better hiring practices, where great companies pursue great programmers rather than forcing developers to navigate broken job boards.
